Open Source in the Age of AI: More Important Than Ever
Why openness matters even more now

Every few years, something comes along that makes the question of open vs. closed feel urgent in a new way. Right now, that thing is AI — and specifically, the large models that are reshaping how software gets written, how decisions get made, and how information gets filtered.
The stakes of openness have never been higher.
Here's the tension: the most capable AI systems today are mostly closed. The weights aren't public. The training data isn't disclosed. The reasoning process is opaque even to the people running them. You interact with these systems through an API, and you take on faith that they're doing what you think they're doing.
That's a significant amount of trust to extend to any system, let alone one that's increasingly embedded in consequential decisions.
Open source AI doesn't solve everything. Open weights don't guarantee interpretability. Releasing a model doesn't automatically tell you what it was trained on or what biases it inherited. Openness is necessary but not sufficient.
But it's still necessary. When a model's weights are public, researchers can study it. When training code is available, problems can be reproduced and debugged. When the community can audit rather than just observe, accountability has somewhere to land.
We've seen this play out already. Some of the most important findings about how large language models behave — how they fail, what they're sensitive to, what they're quietly optimising for — have come from researchers who could actually get inside the thing. That kind of scrutiny is only possible when something is open.
There's also the concentration question. Closed AI systems tend to centralise power. A handful of companies control the most capable models, which means they control a growing share of what's technically possible. Open source is one of the few mechanisms we have to counteract that — to ensure that capability doesn't become permanently gated behind a small number of commercial relationships.
I'm not naive about this. Building frontier AI is genuinely expensive. There are real reasons organisations don't open-source everything. But the direction matters, and the pressure matters. The open source community pushing for transparency, reproducibility, and accessible models is doing important work — not just for software idealists, but for everyone who'll eventually be on the receiving end of these systems.
We spent decades building an open web and then watched it slowly close. I'd rather not repeat that with AI. The time to care about openness is before it's gone.
